title: CH, GW and the geography of tight convex hypersurfaces
speaker: Russell Avdek
abstract: While convex hypersurfaces are well understood in 3d contact topology, we are just starting to explore their basic properties in high dimensions. I will describe how to compute contact homologies (CH) of their neighborhoods, which can be used to infer tightness in any dimension. Then I’ll give a general construction of high-dimensional convex hypersurfaces in the style of Gompf’s fiber sum. For these convex hypersurfaces, relative Gromov-Witten can often compute CH in the style of Diogo-Lisi. We’ll work through some interesting examples.

proof_stages.label: Contact and convex-hypersurface setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes the contact/Liouville viewpoint, reviews tight versus overtwisted contact structures and fillability, and defines a convex hypersurface through its tau-invariant neighborhood, dividing set, and positive/negative Liouville regions.

proof_stages.label: Low-dimensional benchmark and characteristic-number target
proof_stages.summary: Giroux’s criterion and the Thurston-Bennequin inequality are recalled as three-dimensional obstructions, then the four-dimensional characteristic-number relations are stated and the main theorem asserts that every quadruple satisfying them is realized by a connected convex hypersurface with tight neighborhood.

proof_stages.label: Divisor-pair construction of candidate hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: Using Donaldson divisors with symplectomorphic divisor loci, the complements of two closed symplectic manifolds are glued along their common prequantization boundary to produce convex hypersurfaces, including two infinite four-dimensional families that will supply the needed building blocks.

proof_stages.label: Contact homology criterion and bilinearized algebra
proof_stages.summary: Nonvanishing contact homology is invoked as a tightness criterion, and the contact homology of a convex-hypersurface neighborhood is identified on the chain level with a bilinearized DGA built from the two augmentations associated to the positive and negative Liouville regions.

proof_stages.label: Index and relative Gromov-Witten computation of augmentations
proof_stages.summary: For divisor complements, a Morse-Bott perturbation of the prequantization boundary labels Reeb orbits by Morse critical points and covering multiplicities, while holomorphic planes are capped to spheres so that index formulas reduce augmentation counts to relative Gromov-Witten counts with prescribed contact order.

proof_stages.label: Nonzero contact homology for the building blocks
proof_stages.summary: The augmentation computations are applied to the two divisor-pair families: the CP^2 versus CP^1 x CP^1 family has nonzero contact homology exactly for k >= 3, and the positive-genus product family always has nonzero contact homology, giving tight pieces with controlled signature and Euler number.

proof_stages.label: Geography by disjoint unions and contact handles
proof_stages.summary: Copies and orientations of the tight building blocks are chosen to realize the desired signature and Euler number, then contact one- and two-handle attachments connect the hypersurface and adjust Euler characteristic while contact-handle invariance and unit maps keep contact homology nonzero.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and higher-dimensional outlook
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is interpreted as ruling out a Thurston-Bennequin-type characteristic-class obstruction in dimension five, placed alongside related high-dimensional tightness results, and the speaker explains that extending the method would require more higher-dimensional divisor-pair building blocks.

examples.note: Intersecting an algebraic variety in complex affine space with a large ball is used only to make the Liouville/Weinstein background concrete before the main construction.

examples.name: Three-dimensional convex surfaces as multicurves
examples.note: In the case n=1, a convex surface is described by a closed oriented surface with a null-homologous dividing multicurve, illustrating the general positive/negative-region decomposition.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the convex-hypersurface description to the three-dimensional case, explaining how the data becomes a surface with a null-homologous multicurve and how Giroux’s criterion reads in that setting.

passes.reason: The three-dimensional case is the minimal convex-hypersurface base case, where the data reduces to multicurves on surfaces.

examples.name: Divisor-pair construction in real dimension two

examples.name: k=1 lantern-relation divisor pair
examples.note: The quadric in CP^2 and the (2,1) divisor in CP^1 x CP^1 give the McDuff filling/lantern-relation case, later illustrating how an augmentation count can kill contact homology.
examples.engagement_reason: For the k=1 lantern-relation pair, he computes augmentation counts on the two sides, identifies the relevant spheres in P1 x P1, and concludes the differential kills the unit.

examples.name: k=2 tangent-line Gromov-Witten count
examples.note: For the degree-four curve in CP^2 versus the (4,2) divisor in CP^1 x CP^1, the contact-homology differential is computed by counting tangent lines via Riemann-Hurwitz, demonstrating the relative Gromov-Witten technique rather than contributing to the geography proof.
examples.engagement_reason: For k=2, he performs the tangent-line Gromov-Witten count via projection to P1, uses Riemann-Hurwitz to get eight critical points, and derives vanishing contact homology.

passes.reason: The k=2 computation counts non-transverse tangent lines where two intersections coalesce and is used to kill the contact homology.
The boundary k=2 case counts nontransverse tangent fibers whose differential is a unit, again showing contact homology vanishes.
